talk_metadata.talk_title: Mutational Epidemiology and the Role of Promoters in Cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "the vast majority of chemicals that the National Toxicology Program and IARC have classified as being potential carcinogens, they are not mutagenic as far as we can tell"
Speaker noted: "we found a truly resounding negative" when no new mutational signature explained geographic variation in esophageal cancer

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Promotion mechanisms are not captured by mutational signature analysis

key_data_gaps: No functional validation of promoter activity in human cohorts
Lack of longitudinal data linking promoter exposure to cancer incidence

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop computational biomarkers to detect promoter-driven clonal expansion in normal tissues
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted that promoters leave no mutational signature, making them invisible to current genomics approaches
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate epigenomic and clonal dynamics data to infer promoter activity and stratify at‑risk individuals

crispro_opportunity.description: Design prospective cohort studies that incorporate serial tissue sampling to capture promoter‑driven clonal changes
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker emphasized the need for longitudinal monitoring of normal tissues to understand promotion
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO pipelines can model temporal clonal trajectories and suggest optimal sampling intervals

cited_competitors.name: Ludmil Alexandrov
cited_competitors.context: Will discuss further work on mutational signatures at a plenary

unresolved_questions: What specific molecular pathways mediate promotion by non‑mutagenic carcinogens?
Can promoter activity be quantified in human epidemiological cohorts?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Promotion, Selectogens and Clonal Competition in Cancer Initiation
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "the promotion step, in other words, the inflammation, was actually the rate‑limiting step"
Speaker noted: "only three of the carcinogens showed any mutational signatures"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Differential clonal competition not accounted for in current models
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: Speaker admitted that spontaneous KRAS G13R clones disappear over time without clear mechanism
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: Mention of HRAS knockout mice revealing tumors driven by KRAS G13R mutants

key_data_gaps: Translation of mouse promotion findings to human cancer risk

crispro_opportunity.description: Use CrisPRO to model clonal competition dynamics and predict which promoter‑driven clones will expand
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker described how different RAS mutants respond variably to TPA promotion and HRAS knockout
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate multi‑clone ecosystems and identify vulnerable competitive interactions

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop biomarkers for early detection of promoter‑selected clones in normal tissues
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted the presence of numerous mutant clones in normal esophagus that persist for years
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Integrate ultra‑deep sequencing data with CrisPRO to flag expanding clones before tumorigenesis

cited_competitors.name: Jesse Salk / TwinStrand
cited_competitors.context: Provided the deep‑sequencing technology used for rare mutation detection

unresolved_questions: What determines why certain spontaneous RAS mutants disappear while others persist?
Can promoter exposure be mitigated in high‑risk human populations?

talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: Brigham and Women's Hospital / Harvard Medical School
talk_metadata.talk_title: Epigenetic Reprogramming and Combination Therapy in Triple‑Negative Breast Cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "EZH2 and AKT inhibitors together increase the expression of normal mature luminal markers"
Speaker admitted: "we don't think EZH2 or PI3K pathway alterations are useful biomarkers"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Lack of single‑agent activity in TNBC despite high EZH2 expression
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: Speaker noted that ~60% of models responded only to the combination, not to either drug alone

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: EZH2‑mediated epigenetic repression + PI3K/AKT signaling in triple‑negative breast cancer
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: Enrolling all TNBC patients without biomarker‑based stratification
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Baseline GATA3 or mesenchymal signature to predict responders

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed that EZH2 inhibitors lack single‑agent activity, suggesting the core chemistry may be insufficient alone
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Broad TNBC enrollment dilutes signal; need biomarker‑selected population

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed scRNA‑seq data indicating strong basal/mesenchymal signatures in resistant cells, then concluded that EZH2/AKT inhibition can be broadly applied to TNBC without biomarker selection

key_data_gaps: Prospective validation of the predictive gene‑expression signature
Long‑term toxicity of chronic EZH2 inhibition

crispro_opportunity.description: Use CrisPRO to develop a predictive expression signature (mesenchymal vs basal) for EZH2/AKT combination response
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker identified a machine‑learning derived signature that separates sensitive from resistant lines
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate multi‑omics to refine and validate the signature across larger cohorts

crispro_opportunity.description: Explore additional epigenetic partners (e.g., HDAC inhibitors) to overcome resistance in mesenchymal TNBC
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted that resistant lines retain mesenchymal signatures despite treatment
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate triple‑combination networks to prioritize synergistic partners

cited_competitors.name: Ludmil Alexandrov
cited_competitors.context: Will present related mutational signature work at a plenary

unresolved_questions: What upstream pathways maintain the mesenchymal state in resistant TNBC?
Can GATA3 induction be achieved without EZH2 inhibition?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Mechanisms and Subtypes of Cancer Cachexia
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "high‑fat diet actually worsens cancer cachexia"
Speaker noted: "serotonin reuptake inhibitor improves depression but does not improve weight loss"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Prostaglandin E2 production in tumor driven by high‑fat diet exacerbates wasting via vagal signaling
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: Patients receiving SSRI showed improved mood but no change in body weight or survival
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: High‑fat diet increases tumor‑derived prostaglandin E2, worsening cachexia

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Identification of inflammatory vs non‑inflammatory cachexia subtypes
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: Broad inclusion of all advanced cancer patients without stratification by inflammatory biomarkers
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Baseline GDF15/IL‑6 levels to separate inflamed vs non‑inflamed cachexia
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Potential underpowering for subgroup efficacy analyses

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: Serotonin reuptake inhibitors for cachexia‑related depression
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker demonstrated that SSRIs improve depressive symptoms but do not affect the core wasting phenotype
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Targeting mood alone does not address muscle/fat loss, limiting therapeutic impact

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that correcting serotonin deficiency rescues depression in mice, yet concluded that this does not improve overall cachexia outcomes

key_data_gaps: Intervention studies that simultaneously address both mood and metabolic wasting
Long‑term survival benefit of anti‑inflammatory vs metabolic therapies

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop CrisPRO‑driven multi‑omics signatures to separate inflammatory (high GDF15/IL‑6) from non‑inflammatory cachexia patients
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker identified two patient clusters (red inflammatory, orange non‑inflammatory) with distinct outcomes
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate cytokine, metabolite, and transcriptomic data to generate a predictive cachexia subtype classifier

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify agents that simultaneously modulate brain‑derived appetite pathways and tumor‑derived metabolic drivers
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker described separate mechanisms: brain serotonin for depression and tumor‑derived prostaglandin E2 for wasting
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model cross‑tissue signaling networks to prioritize dual‑target combos (e.g., SSRIs + COX‑2 inhibitors)

cited_competitors.name: Ludmil Alexandrov
cited_competitors.context: Mentioned as speaker at a future plenary on mutational signatures

unresolved_questions: Can early detection of the kynurenine/serotonin imbalance predict severe depression in cachectic patients?
What therapeutic strategies can simultaneously reverse metabolic wasting and improve appetite?
